summary: The post examines the statistical challenges the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau faces when measuring disparate impact in auto lending discrimination cases. O'Neil argues that even if race data were directly available, numerous methodological choices around pooling data by location, time, and dealer create uncertainty. Banks exploit this ambiguity by arguing for whichever statistical model minimizes their liability. She advocates for standardized measurement approaches not because they'd be perfect, but because they would prevent gaming between competing models and ensure consistent treatment across cases.
key_points: Proving individual racism is nearly impossible; disparate impact is inherently a statistical question comparing pools of minority vs. white applicants
Pooling data increases statistical power but masks variation across locations, time periods, and individual dealers
Separating data to account for these factors reduces sample sizes and increases error bars, creating a fundamental tradeoff
Banks game the system by always arguing for whichever model minimizes their liability
Standardization of measurement methods would reduce gaming even if the chosen standard isn't perfect
money_quotes: It has to be said that it's difficult if not impossible to prove an individual case of racism.
The good thing about pooling is that you have more data and thus smaller error bars; it's easier to make the case that disparate impact has taken place beyond a reasonable statistical doubt.
Because there are various models that could be used, there's always one model that ends up costing the bank the least. They will always argue for that one.
They even have an expert consultant who works both for the CFPB and the banks and helps them game the models in this way.
I'd suggest we have some standards for measuring disparate impact, so that the 'gaming between models' comes to an end.
Not because it's perfect, but because it will prevent banks from complaining that other banks get treated better.
key_insight: The absence of standardized statistical methods for measuring discrimination allows banks to game enforcement by always advocating for whichever model minimizes their liability.
